question: Tell us about an extracurricular you came close to quitting. What kept you in it?
lookingFor: Honest reflection on a real low point, and evidence of a deliberate, reasoned choice to stay rather than inertia.

bullets: Name the activity and describe the specific moment of doubt genuinely
Explain what internal or external factor shifted your thinking
Connect what staying taught you about yourself
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: By the end of my first year in the school orchestra I was ready to stop — I was the weakest player in the section and rehearsals felt more like embarrassment than music. What stopped me was a conversation with the conductor, who told me she'd kept the chair open for exactly that reason. I realised I was measuring myself against people who'd played since age six, not against where I'd started. I stayed, moved up a part the following year, and learned something I still use: progress is almost never visible from inside the slump.

commonMistakes: Choosing a trivial activity where quitting had no real cost
Framing the story as external pressure forcing them to stay, not a genuine decision
Skipping the reflection and just saying 'I'm glad I stayed'
followUps: In hindsight, would it have been okay to quit? How do you know when quitting is the right call?

question: Describe a moment your leadership style didn't work. What did you change in the moment?
lookingFor: Genuine awareness of a personal leadership blind spot, with evidence of real-time adaptation rather than post-hoc rationalisation.

bullets: Name the specific situation and what approach you were taking
Identify the moment you realised it wasn't working and why
Describe the concrete pivot you made and what it produced
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I was running a student charity drive and I'd mapped out every task in detail — I gave people job descriptions, timelines, the lot. Three weeks in, nothing was moving and the team felt stifled. A teammate told me bluntly that people didn't want a project manager, they wanted to own something. I stopped the next meeting, scrapped the task list, and asked everyone to pick an area they cared about and design their own approach. We raised more than our target. I still love structure, but I've learned to hold it more loosely when I'm leading volunteers.

commonMistakes: Describing a failure that was actually someone else's fault
Claiming they immediately became a perfect leader afterwards
Refusing to name a genuine flaw — pivoting to a strength in disguise
followUps: How do you decide which leadership style fits which situation now?

question: What's the most concrete change you made to an organisation or team you were part of — and how did it outlast you?
lookingFor: Evidence that the student built something durable, not just performed a role. Looks for initiative and follow-through.

bullets: Describe the specific gap or problem they saw and why others hadn't acted on it
Explain what they built, changed, or introduced concretely
Show that the change persisted or was adopted by others after they moved on
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: When I joined the school's Model UN team there was no onboarding for new members — experienced delegates would just throw you in at a conference and you'd sink or swim. I wrote a short handbook and ran two practice sessions for newcomers before our next tournament. The year after I left, the next captain told me they'd run the same sessions with my notes, and the year after that they added a buddy system on top. I'm proud of that more than any award I won competing, because it changed how the team works, not just what it did.

commonMistakes: Describing participation or hard work with no lasting change
Claiming impact they can't verify or demonstrate
Focusing on personal achievement rather than what they left behind
followUps: If you had another year in the role, what would you have built next?

question: Most students are involved in several activities. Which one did you deliberately scale back on, and was it the right call?
lookingFor: Honest cost-benefit reasoning about time and energy — shows maturity and intentional choice-making.

bullets: Name the activity and the specific reason they pulled back
Acknowledge what they gave up, not just what they gained
Reflect honestly on whether the trade-off was right
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: In my penultimate year I was playing in two sports teams and editing the school paper, and something had to give when I took on the lead role for the science fair. I stopped playing the second sport — I kept it to the one I loved most. I do miss it; we had a good team that year and I wasn't there for it. But the science project turned into something I'm genuinely proud of and probably wouldn't have without the space. I think it was the right call, though I'd tell a younger version of myself to make the decision earlier instead of grinding through a month of doing everything badly.

commonMistakes: Insisting they never had to make trade-offs
Choosing an activity they clearly didn't value, so there was no real cost
Turning the answer into a brag about how much they do
followUps: How do you decide now what deserves your time?

subtopic: Conflict within a team you led
question: You're in charge of a team and two members are openly clashing. Walk us through exactly what you'd do.
lookingFor: A structured, human approach to interpersonal conflict — shows emotional intelligence and leadership maturity.

bullets: Describe how they'd address it promptly without being reactive
Show they'd speak to each person separately before bringing them together
Explain how they'd refocus on the shared goal rather than picking a winner
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I'd start by talking to each person separately — in the same meeting you can't hear anyone, you're just managing performance. I'd ask each of them what they actually need from the situation, not who's right. Then I'd bring them together with a shared question to focus on: 'What does the team need to move forward?' rather than 'who's wrong?' I've found that most conflict in student teams isn't personality — it's unclear expectations or workload that feels unfair. Once you name that, you can actually fix it. If it continued after that, I'd separate their work streams and be honest with them that the team couldn't afford the disruption.

commonMistakes: Jumping straight to a group confrontation or public resolution
Avoiding the conflict entirely and hoping it resolves itself
Picking a side immediately rather than diagnosing the root cause
followUps: Has this actually happened to you? How did it go?

question: Tell us about something you did consistently that gave you no obvious status or recognition — why did you keep doing it?
lookingFor: Evidence of intrinsic motivation and genuine values, not just resume-building behaviour.

bullets: Name an activity that is genuinely unglamorous or invisible
Describe what the internal reward was, honestly
Explain what it tells you about what you value
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: For two years I ran a weekly reading session at a local primary school — an hour on Thursday afternoons, just sitting with kids who struggled. There was no certificate, it didn't count for anything, and some weeks a child would just draw instead of read. But the week one boy read a full page without stopping, unprompted, I felt something I've rarely felt anywhere else. I kept going because of moments like that, and because I liked who I was in that room. I think it told me that working on something slow and unspectacular with someone who needs it is actually the kind of contribution I find meaningful.

commonMistakes: Choosing something that actually had public recognition and downplaying it
Listing the activity without explaining the internal motivation
Sounding preachy or virtuous rather than honest
followUps: Would you still do it if it actively hurt your university application?

question: Was your leadership role something you earned or something you were handed? What difference did that make?
lookingFor: Self-awareness about whether the student has tested themselves versus benefited from position — and honest reflection on what they did with whatever they were given.

bullets: Be honest about the route into the role rather than inflating it
Reflect on what the circumstances required of them differently than if they'd built it
Show what they actually made of the role, regardless of how they got it
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: If I'm honest, the captaincy was partly handed to me — I was the most experienced person left after the previous captain graduated, and no one else put their hand up. It would be easy to overstate how much I earned it. What I did earn was keeping it: the team had drifted in the previous year and I had to rebuild trust with people who weren't sure I was the right person. The route in being a bit accidental made me more conscious of proving I belonged there. I think that awareness was actually useful — it meant I never felt I could coast.

commonMistakes: Claiming they totally earned every leadership role with no self-examination
Downplaying the role entirely in false modesty
Failing to reflect on how the circumstances shaped what leadership looked like
followUps: What would you have done differently if you'd had to campaign for the role?

question: Describe a time you influenced a group's direction without having any formal authority. How did you do it?
lookingFor: Understanding of how to persuade and move people without positional power — shows maturity and social intelligence.

bullets: Set up the situation clearly — why they had no authority and what was at stake
Describe the specific approach they used, not just 'I spoke up'
Reflect on what made the influence work or what the limits were
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Our year group was organising an end-of-year event and the committee kept pushing for something expensive that most students couldn't afford. I wasn't on the committee, but I started asking around quietly and realised about a third of my class felt the same way but hadn't said so. I brought a short informal survey to the next open meeting with the numbers on it — not to argue, just to put the data on the table. The committee changed course. What worked wasn't making it a confrontation; it was making it a legitimate piece of information they hadn't had. That distinction felt important.

commonMistakes: Describing a situation where they actually had formal power or standing
Claiming influence with no explanation of the mechanism
Making it sound manipulative rather than persuasive
followUps: What would you have done if no one had listened?

question: Tell us about a decision you made as a leader that your team didn't like. How did you handle the pushback?
lookingFor: Courage to make principled decisions and genuine skill in communicating them — not just stubbornness or capitulation.

bullets: Describe the decision clearly and why it was right even though it was unpopular
Show they gave the team a genuine hearing rather than dismissing them
Explain how they communicated the reasoning and what happened next
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: We were entering a regional debate competition and I decided to cut one of our longest-serving members from the lineup because another student had a much stronger argument for that round. He was furious, and some of the team were uncomfortable about it. I met with him privately before announcing it, explained my reasoning specifically — not just 'she's better' but why that match-up mattered — and told him I wanted him as our coach for the round instead. He wasn't happy at first. We won the round, and he later told me he understood the call. I think the key was being honest and direct rather than apologising my way around the decision.

commonMistakes: Describing a decision that had no real cost to them as a leader
Capitulating to pressure and calling that leadership
Showing no empathy for the people who disagreed
followUps: Looking back, is there a way you could have handled the pushback better?

question: Tell us about something new you tried to start or launch that didn't take off. What did you learn?
lookingFor: Honest account of a genuine initiative that failed, with clear-eyed reflection on why — not a reframed success.

bullets: Describe what they tried to build and why
Explain honestly what went wrong — not just circumstances but their own missteps
Name the specific lesson they took from it
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I tried to start a philosophy discussion club in my lower sixth year. I put up posters, booked a room, turned up — six students came the first week, two the second, zero the third. I'd completely skipped the step of checking whether anyone actually wanted this beyond my own enthusiasm. I'd built something for an imagined audience. What I took from it is that if you want to start something, spend two weeks having conversations before you spend five minutes on a poster. I applied that to everything I did after, including a mentoring scheme later that year that actually ran.

commonMistakes: Picking something that 'failed' but was actually quietly successful
Blaming the school, timing, or other people entirely
Stopping at 'it failed' with no analysis of their own role in it
followUps: If you had another shot at it with what you know now, what would you do first?

question: Have you ever had to re-energise a team that had lost momentum or morale? What did you actually do?
lookingFor: Concrete, human strategies for rebuilding team energy — not generic cheerleader answers.

bullets: Diagnose what specifically caused the low morale, not just describe it
Describe the specific action they took — not a general pep talk
Show the outcome and what they'd do differently
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: We had a rough run of fundraising events that flopped and the team stopped turning up for planning sessions — morale was genuinely low. I didn't call a motivational meeting. Instead, I scaled everything right down: I suggested we run just one small event we could actually control, and I asked each person to own a single decision within it. We raised almost nothing, but we executed something cleanly, and for the first time in months people left feeling like a team. The lesson was that rebuilding momentum is about small, achievable wins — not speeches. You re-energise people by letting them succeed at something modest.

commonMistakes: Relying on a rousing speech with no concrete follow-up
Blaming the team's attitude without examining structural causes
Not having a real example and speaking hypothetically throughout
followUps: How do you spot early when a team is losing momentum, before it becomes a crisis?

question: You've done a lot. Which single activity has shaped you the most, and what specifically did it change?
lookingFor: The ability to distinguish meaningful depth from surface-level participation — and to name a precise, honest change in who they are.

bullets: Choose one activity confidently and explain why it stands above the others
Name a specific, concrete way it changed them — not a generic 'I grew as a person'
Show why the change has lasted
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The thing that changed me most wasn't the captaincy or the competition wins — it was three years of volunteering as a Samaritans listener on a youth helpline. What it specifically changed is how I listen. Before, I used to listen while formulating my response. Now I actually wait until someone is finished. That sounds small but it changes almost every conversation I have. The training was intense and the calls were hard, but the skill it built is one I use constantly, and I think it's made me a fundamentally better collaborator and a more patient thinker.

commonMistakes: Hedging and naming two or three activities instead of choosing
Naming a prestigious activity they can't speak about with real depth
Describing the activity rather than the change it made
followUps: What would you be like without that experience? Is that a fair question?

question: Tell us about a time you had to work closely with someone whose approach was completely different from yours. How did you make it work?
lookingFor: Genuine flexibility and curiosity toward different working styles — not just tolerance.

bullets: Describe the actual difference in approach concretely
Explain the specific adjustment they made — not just 'we talked it out'
Reflect on what they learned from the other person's approach
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: My co-editor on the school magazine planned nothing — she'd arrive the day before print with three half-written features and somehow make it work. I arrive at every deadline with a spreadsheet. For the first two issues I was constantly anxious and we clashed constantly. Eventually I stopped trying to convert her and instead proposed clear non-negotiable checkpoints — just two — with complete freedom on how she got there. She hit both. More importantly, I started noticing her instinct for what would actually be a good story was faster and more accurate than my planned approach. Now I set structure and try to leave the creative path open. I'm a better editor for it.

commonMistakes: Describing the difference as a conflict they 'fixed' by getting the other person to change
Framing the other person's approach as simply wrong
Pretending there was no real tension
followUps: Did working with them change how you prefer to work, or just how you adapt?

question: If you started something from scratch — a club, event, or project — what was the hardest part of building it from nothing?
lookingFor: Real founder-level reflection on the unglamorous work of starting: recruiting, legitimacy, sustaining early momentum.

bullets: Name the specific hardest problem — not 'it was hard generally'
Describe what they actually did about it
Reflect on what surprised them about building from scratch
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The hardest part of starting our school's first Model UN chapter wasn't the admin or even getting teachers on board — it was convincing the first ten students to take a chance on something with no track record. People didn't trust it yet, because it had no history. I realised I was asking people to believe in me as much as the idea, and I hadn't given them much reason to. I ended up asking the five people I'd worked with before to be the founding group — not because they were the most interested in MUN, but because they trusted me enough to show up. Their presence got the next fifteen through the door. The lesson was: trust transfers. Your first followers are the product.

commonMistakes: Underplaying the difficulty by saying 'it just came together'
Focusing on logistics rather than the human challenges of early adoption
Not distinguishing this experience from joining something that already existed
followUps: What would you do differently in the first month if you started it again?

subtopic: When your values clashed with a team
question: Has there been a moment in any of your activities where what the team was doing conflicted with something you believed was wrong? What did you do?
lookingFor: Evidence the student has genuine values and the willingness to act on them — even at social cost.

bullets: Describe a genuine values conflict, not just a preference difference
Show they raised it rather than staying silent or quietly going along
Reflect honestly on how it landed and whether they'd do it the same way again
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: We were raising money for a trip by running a sponsored silence and one of the team suggested we anonymously share a photo mocking a student from a rival school to drive engagement online. A few people laughed it off. I said I wasn't comfortable with it and explained why — we'd be doing something unkind to someone for a laugh, and I didn't want our campaign built on that. There was a slightly uncomfortable pause. They dropped the idea. I don't think I was a hero for saying it; I just didn't want to be part of something mean and say nothing. What surprised me was how quickly it ended — I think a few others were uncomfortable too and were waiting for someone to name it.

commonMistakes: Describing a values conflict that was actually a minor preference disagreement
Claiming they always speak up without any reflection on the cost
Making themselves the unambiguous moral protagonist with no nuance
followUps: Was there a situation where you stayed silent when you shouldn't have?

question: If you held a representative role — student council, house captain, team captain — describe a moment when you had to prioritise the group's needs over your own preferences.
lookingFor: Understanding that representative leadership means subordinating personal preference to collective need — not just enjoying status.

bullets: Describe the specific tension between what they wanted and what the group needed
Explain the decision clearly and how they made it
Reflect on what that taught them about what leadership actually is
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: As student council president I had to decide how to allocate a small discretionary budget. I wanted to put it toward a philosophy lecture series — genuinely, my favourite idea. But every piece of feedback I'd gathered pointed to a common room refurbishment as the priority for most students. I put it toward the refurbishment. It felt like the right call but it stung a little. What I realised is that the role came with a version of mandate — I'd been chosen to represent people, not to optimise for my own taste. That's not a small distinction. I think I'm still working out what it means to lead something where your job is to amplify voices, not just project your own.

commonMistakes: Confusing leadership with getting what you want faster
Pretending the personal sacrifice cost them nothing
Failing to reflect on the structural meaning of representation
followUps: How did you communicate the decision to the students who voted for you?

question: Tell us about an extracurricular you joined specifically because it scared you or felt outside your natural strength. How did it go?
lookingFor: Intentional growth mindset — evidence the student deliberately seeks discomfort rather than retreating to known strengths.

bullets: Be honest about what specifically was frightening or unfamiliar
Describe how they performed — without inflating success
Reflect on what they actually gained, even if they weren't good at it
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I joined the school's improv comedy group in my fourth year specifically because public speaking terrified me and I thought structured debate practice wasn't fixing it — the fear was specifically about being spontaneous in front of people. I was genuinely bad at improv for months. There's nowhere to hide in it. But by the end of the year I'd learned something structural: most of my social anxiety came from trying to be clever rather than just responding. Improv forces you to accept whatever's in front of you and move forward. I'm still not a natural performer, but I'm a much more relaxed speaker now, and that came from deliberately being bad at something for a while.

commonMistakes: Naming something that turned out to be an easy win — no real growth
Focusing entirely on the success without the discomfort
Choosing something peripheral that had no real stakes for them
followUps: What's the next thing outside your comfort zone you want to try at university?

question: Have you ever taught or mentored someone younger? What did that experience teach you about yourself?
lookingFor: Insight from the experience of teaching — that explaining something to others deepens your own understanding and reveals your patience and communication.

bullets: Describe the mentoring relationship specifically, not in passing
Name what was genuinely hard about it
Reflect on what the experience revealed about the student, not just the mentee
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I spent a year as a peer tutor for GCSE maths and for the first few sessions I was essentially re-teaching my own notes at people. It didn't land. One student asked me 'but why does that rule work?' and I realised I didn't actually know — I'd memorised it. That sent me back to really understand the underlying principle, and once I could explain why, not just how, my tutoring started to work. Teaching taught me that I had gaps I didn't know I had, and that understanding something well enough to explain it simply is a completely different skill from performing well on an exam. It's a standard I try to hold myself to now.

commonMistakes: Focusing on what they gave the mentee, not what they learned themselves
Presenting themselves as immediately effective and naturally gifted at teaching
Describing a very short or low-stakes instance
followUps: What's the hardest thing to teach to someone else, in your experience?

question: Are you more motivated by competition or by collaboration? Give us a concrete example that shows why.
lookingFor: Self-aware, nuanced answer — not an automatic 'collaboration is best' but honest reflection grounded in a real example.

bullets: Take a position rather than hedging — say which and why
Back it with a specific moment, not just a general claim
Acknowledge the limits or downsides of their preference
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Honestly, I perform better under competition — and I've learned to be honest about that even when it doesn't sound as good in an answer. The year we had a rival school running a similar science project to ours, I worked harder than at any other point in my school career. Knowing there was a benchmark sharpened me in a way internal motivation alone doesn't. That said, I've seen the shadow side of that: I've been in competitive environments where people stopped sharing useful information because they were tracking against each other. I try to channel the drive without letting it make me territorial. I think collaboration makes better outcomes; competition makes me personally show up. I want both.

commonMistakes: Giving a safe 'I love collaboration' answer with no real substance
Claiming both equally without committing to either
No concrete example to ground the claim
followUps: Describe a time competition got the better of you — when did it stop being useful?

subtopic: Activity that connected to your studies
question: Has anything you did outside the classroom ever changed how you thought about something you were studying? Tell us about the connection.
lookingFor: Evidence of genuine intellectual synthesis — that the student's extracurricular life and academic life are in conversation, not siloed.

bullets: Name the activity and the subject with specificity
Describe the precise moment of connection — what changed and how
Show that the insight moved in both directions, not just 'activity confirmed my studies'
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I play chess competitively and I thought it was completely separate from school until I studied game theory in economics. Suddenly I could see that the entire strategic framework I used intuitively — anticipating my opponent's move before committing to my own — had formal mathematical structure. But the connection worked the other way too: game theory gave me language for why some of my chess instincts were wrong. I'd been treating some positions as zero-sum when they weren't, and the theory helped me see it. That crossover made me realise I learn better when I'm doing something with knowledge, not just receiving it.

commonMistakes: Naming a connection so obvious it needs no reflection ('I like sport and studied biology')
Describing the activity without actually explaining the intellectual link
Treating the extracurricular as merely illustrative of the subject, not genuinely connected
followUps: What's a connection you suspect exists but haven't fully worked out yet?

question: Tell us about a period when external circumstances — injury, family, school pressure — made it hard to keep up with something you cared about. How did you handle the tension?
lookingFor: Honest reflection on how the student managed real competing demands — not a polished triumph narrative, but genuine evidence of prioritisation and coping.

bullets: Be specific about the external pressure and how significant it genuinely was
Describe the actual choice they made — what they maintained and what they let go
Reflect on whether they handled it well or what they'd do differently
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: My father had a serious health scare in my GCSE mock year and I was taking on more at home than I'd told anyone at school. I was also running the school newspaper at the time, which I wasn't willing to give up entirely because it was the one thing keeping me feeling like myself. What I did — badly at first — was try to maintain the same level of output and just absorb the cost. Eventually I told the deputy editor what was happening and shared the workload properly. The paper ran fine. I realised I'd been treating asking for help as failure, which is a habit I've had to actively work on since.

commonMistakes: Choosing a pressure that had no real stakes and describing it dramatically
Presenting themselves as having handled it perfectly with no cost
Avoiding the emotional honesty and staying entirely at the surface
followUps: What would you tell a friend who was in the same situation?

question: As a leader, how did you identify people with potential and bring them on? Tell us about a specific person.
lookingFor: Evidence that the student led by developing others, not just by performing themselves — the difference between a leader and a high achiever.

bullets: Describe how they spotted potential — what specifically made them notice this person
Explain what they did to develop them, not just encourage them
Reflect on the outcome and what it taught them about leadership
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: In the drama club I noticed one of the quieter members in rehearsal had a really precise instinct for what wasn't working in a scene, but she never said anything in the group. I started asking for her opinion one-on-one after sessions, and it became clear she had a natural eye for direction. I suggested she shadow me for one production and then run the second act of the next one herself. She was nervous but she did it, and it was better than mine. Watching someone grow into something they didn't know they could do is a different kind of satisfaction from performing well yourself — and honestly it made me question whether I wanted to direct or whether I just hadn't noticed there was a better option in the room.

commonMistakes: Describing generic encouragement without specific development actions
Making the story about themselves rather than the other person
Choosing someone who was already obviously talented and simply inviting them in
followUps: Has that person gone on to do something with it? How does that feel?

question: When you left a leadership role or stepped back from an activity, how did you handle the handover? What did you leave for whoever came next?
lookingFor: Maturity and responsibility toward the organisation beyond the student's own tenure — evidence they thought beyond themselves.

bullets: Describe what they specifically did to prepare the handover — not just 'I briefed them'
Show they thought about what the successor would need, not what they'd want to preserve
Reflect on what they'd do better if they could do it again
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: When I handed over the editorship of the paper I wrote what I called a 'what I wish I'd known' document — not a policy manual, but an honest account of the two or three things that had caught me off guard in my first term. I also introduced my successor to three external contacts I'd built — journalists and a local editor who'd been useful to us — because those relationships had taken me a year to build and I didn't want them to disappear with me. Looking back, I should have involved her in decisions for the final month, not just briefed her at the end. A handover document is fine, but watching decisions happen in real time is better preparation.

commonMistakes: Claiming they handed over comprehensively with no gaps
Focusing on what they preserved rather than what the next person needed
Treating the exit as a formality rather than a responsibility
followUps: Did the person who came after you do it differently? Was that okay?

question: Tell us about involvement you had in your wider community — outside school. What drew you there and what did you contribute?
lookingFor: Evidence of civic engagement and initiative beyond the structured school environment — shows the student operates independently.

bullets: Name a specific community involvement, not a school-organised trip
Explain what drew them to it — the honest motivation
Describe the actual contribution, not just attendance
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I spent a year helping run a free Saturday coding session for kids in my neighbourhood through a local library programme. I got involved because my younger cousin couldn't afford the coding camps that were on offer and I thought that was wrong. I designed about half the curriculum from scratch because the existing material assumed access to personal laptops, and most of the kids were using library computers with restricted software. I'm not sure I was always a great teacher but I understood the specific constraints those kids had in a way that made the sessions more useful than they'd been. I kept going because the problem felt solvable in a way that motivated me.

commonMistakes: Describing a school-mandated volunteering requirement as personal civic engagement
Overstating the contribution while downplaying the structural support behind it
Failing to explain the honest motivation — sounding performatively virtuous
followUps: Did you encounter a problem with the community work you didn't know how to solve?

question: Has your definition of success in an activity changed over time? Walk us through how it shifted.
lookingFor: Mature, reflective evidence of growth — the student can articulate a genuine change in what they value, not just what they've achieved.

bullets: Name the activity and the earlier definition of success clearly
Describe the experience or moment that shifted it
Articulate the new definition — and show it's genuinely different, not just more sophisticated phrasing
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: When I started swimming competitively, success was entirely about personal bests — time on the clock. That's clean and measurable and it drove me hard for three years. It shifted after I missed a qualifying cut by two seconds and spent the next month unable to get back in the water. I realised that if the only thing left when the times stopped improving was disappointment, I'd built something too brittle. I went back with a different question: what do I actually love about this? Turns out it was the early mornings before anyone was there, the physical certainty of training well, and a handful of close teammates. Success stopped being the clock and started being whether I'd shown up fully and stayed curious about the sport. Ironically, my times improved after that shift — I think because I stopped racing myself anxiously.

commonMistakes: Claiming they always had the mature definition and never needed to change
Describing a shift that's trivially minor or obviously prompted by the question
Leaving the new definition so vague it sounds like a PR answer
followUps: Do you think the earlier, narrower definition of success was wrong, or just incomplete?
